section_path: Bumble Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
paragraphs.text: October 2025

section_path: Bumble Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Bumble welcomes the European Commission’s initiative to strengthen consumer protection through the upcoming Digital Fairness Act (DFA). The Bumble platform brings people closer to love by enabling them to build healthy relationships. Founded in 2014, Bumble was one of the first dating apps built with women at the center. Bumble was built on the philosophy that when you make dating better for women, you make it better for everyone. Our platform is designed to foster meaningful, authentic connections, with our community playing a vital role in creating that environment. We hold our members accountable for their actions and strive to provide them with an experience free from hate, aggression, or bullying. As a platform founded on respect, safety, and accountability, we are committed to empowering our members and ensuring fair, equitable online experiences.

section_path: Bumble Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: We share the Commission’s ambition to tackle harmful practices in the digital environment. However, the measures the Commission proposes should be specific to the unfair practices and their industry. Bumble has consistently demonstrated a commitment to ethical design, transparency, and user safety, often going beyond industry standards. For instance, Bumble pioneered the introduction of the Snooze feature, designed to encourage users to take a break when their screen time was becoming too much. The feature allows people to pause their activity on the app (without losing their matches) and prioritise their mental wellbeing at any moment while using our app.

section_path: Bumble Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: It is important to recognise that the Digital Services Act (DSA) has already introduced a comprehensive framework to address structural risks online including deceptive or manipulative design, addictive features that exploit consumer vulnerabilities. Existing consumer protection regulations in the European Union (EU) provide protections to consumers from challenges from online services including misleading marketing by online influencers, as well as difficulties in managing digital contracts. Any new obligations under the DFA should therefore avoid duplication and focus on targeted gaps within specific industries where consumers remain at risk.

section_path: Bumble Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act
Introduction
paragraphs.text: Bumble Response | Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act | October 2025

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: We support the Commission's focus on addressing harmful design practices. However, we encourage the DFA to recognise that platforms' incentives and design choices are shaped by their underlying economic models and target audience, which might evolve over time. A rigid- or one-size-fits-all framework risks constraining innovation and discouraging firms from adapting to changing consumer preferences or market conditions.

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Bumble currently operates a subscription-based model and does not adopt practices to retain users on the platform. Our success lies in helping users build meaningful connections and ultimately leave the app, rather than maximizing time spent online. Nevertheless, it is important that the DFA provides sufficient flexibility so that companies considering alternative revenue streams, such as advertising, are not penalised for adjusting their models, provided they maintain a commitment to user safety and ethical design.

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: We support strong and consistent consumer protections but encourage a proportionate, risk-based approach in the Digital Fairness Act. While all traders should be covered, the scope and intensity of obligations should reflect a service's scale and potential consumer impact. A tiered model, similar to the DSA, would uphold fairness while ensuring regulatory effort is focused where risk is highest and burdens remain proportionate under Article 5 of the Treaty on European Union.

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Therefore, we recommend that the DFA:

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Take account of economic models when assessing design practices, recognising the different incentives created by subscription-based and advertising-driven approaches.

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Follow a proportionate, risk-based approach, ensuring the scope and intensity of obligations reflect a service's scale and potential consumer impact.

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Provide flexibility so that obligations remain aligned with evolving business models, ensuring firms can adapt without regulatory uncertainty;

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Recognise services that voluntarily avoid attention-capture mechanisms, irrespective of the revenue model;

section_path: Bumble
Business models and regulatory adaptability
paragraphs.text: Build on existing DSA safeguards, filling gaps without unnecessary duplication.

section_path: Harmonise Subscription Rules Across the EU
paragraphs.text: Bumble supports a straightforward, transparent and fair subscription ecosystem. We agree with the Commission's intention to address concerns around hidden terms, unfair renewals, and burdensome cancellation processes.

section_path: Harmonise Subscription Rules Across the EU
paragraphs.text: By contrast, Bumble's is aimed at users aged 18 and over and its subscription model is designed around clarity, consent, and user control. Our business model aligns with user success: we thrive when people find meaningful connections, not when they are locked into unwanted subscriptions. Paid subscription and features within our apps are offered with pre-purchase information which make clear to members the benefits contained within a subscription, the duration the subscription will last for and the total cost for that period in the members local currency.

section_path: Harmonise Subscription Rules Across the EU
paragraphs.text: We recommend that the DFA:

section_path: Harmonise Subscription Rules Across the EU
paragraphs.text: Harmonise renewal and cancellation rules across the EU to prevent fragmentation;

section_path: Harmonise Subscription Rules Across the EU
paragraphs.text: Focus enforcement on harmful practices, while recognising responsible actors already exceeding baseline standards;

section_path: Harmonise Subscription Rules Across the EU
paragraphs.text: Build on the DSA and existing consumer protection frameworks rather than over regulating compliant services.

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: We support measures to tackle manipulative interface designs ("dark patterns"), which erode consumer trust. At the same time, overly broad definitions risk discouraging legitimate design choices, such as safety nudges or onboarding prompts, that protect users and enhance their experience.

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: Bumble's focus is to bring people closer to love by enabling them to build healthy and authentic relationships. Bumble aims to create meaningful online experiences that lead to real connections in the offline world. We design our products to empower users to make informed choices about their digital experiences. We use transparent design and in-app communication to explain how features, subscriptions, and upgrades work. Our interfaces avoid coercive or deceptive practices, and our subscription flows provide clear information about prices, renewals, and cancellation.

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: While we acknowledge concerns about "dark patterns" and "addictive design", we caution that these concepts require precise definitions. Some interface features such as recommendations being based on user preferences are inherent to the dating industry, whilst others like reminders or engagement prompts serve legitimate purposes, including promoting safety or facilitating meaningful connections. For example, Bumble encourages members to complete their profiles through thoughtful in-app prompts and reminders. These gentle nudges help users present themselves authentically, leading to more meaningful matches and healthier connections, to foster intentional relationships rather than maximise screen time.

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: We therefore encourage the Commission to adopt a risk-based, contextual approach distinguishing manipulative practices from legitimate user experience optimisation. Moreover, personalisation is a core element of user experience on digital platforms, including dating services. Our matching algorithms and content recommendations are designed to enhance relevance and safety, not to exploit vulnerabilities.

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: We encourage the Commission to differentiate between unfair profiling that manipulates consumer choices and responsible personalisation that supports user intent. Regulatory clarity on this distinction would help businesses innovate responsibly while maintaining consumer protection. We recommend that the DFA:

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: Maintain consistency with the DSA by using existing definitions of dark patterns

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: Differentiate between unfair profiling and responsible personalisation supporting user intent.

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: Target any enforcement actions against deceptive and coercive practices;

section_path: Define Dark Patterns by intent and harm & Responsible Personalisation
paragraphs.text: Acknowledge ethical, safety-driven design as part of responsible platform behaviour.

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
paragraphs.text: Bumble is an adults-only platform, with strict age controls to prevent minors from accessing our services. Our product design and communications are directed exclusively at adults seeking respectful, consensual connections. This is stated in our Terms and Conditions, Privacy Policy and Community Guidelines. We encourage policymakers to highlight and support platforms that already integrate robust age-appropriate access controls as standard practice.

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
paragraphs.text: Any users discovered to be younger than 18 will be blocked from accessing the platform. To do this, Bumble Inc. continually invests in a robust age assurance and verification system that is a combination of technical, automated, and manual protection measures used to

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
paragraphs.text: restrict access to content from those not appropriately aged for our products. These systems can be categorised into proactive and reactive measures, and consist of both automated and human moderation.

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
paragraphs.text: Our apps are marked as mature in app stores and date of birth is mandatory to register, including use of third-party verification at point of registration. In addition to self-declaration, we use facial analysis technology and interference models to estimate a person's age. We reserve the right to ask for identification and ban accounts from our platforms if we have reason to believe an account might belong to somebody under the age of 18. We proactively deploy tools including text and image-based detection (powered by machine learning and computer vision models), hash matching, and keyword-based detection to take automated actions on accounts or flag profiles for human moderation review. To strengthen our member authenticity measures, we have introduced voluntary ID verification in Q2 2025 as an opt-in option for our members, which requires a scan of a valid government-issued ID to verify member age and identity. Our members can make reports of underage users they suspect to be on our platforms, both in-app and via online contact form. These reports are reviewed, classified and actioned by our moderation teams. In any and all of these instances, once identified, we block device IDs for underage users to prevent completion of registration or to remove access to our apps.

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
Influencer Marketing and Advertising Transparency
paragraphs.text: Bumble works with influencers and brand partners across the EU to ensure content is transparent and in line with local requirements. We comply with existing consumer protection and advertising laws and ensure that all sponsored content is clearly identifiable as such.

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
Influencer Marketing and Advertising Transparency
paragraphs.text: We support EU-wide alignment on influencer responsibility and transparency, provided that new obligations are consistent with the DSA's rules on advertising transparency. Harmonisation in this area would prevent fragmentation and create a level playing field for both EU and non-EU businesses.

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Bumble strongly supports the Commission's ambition to ensure fairness, transparency, and accountability in the digital marketplace. The DFA is an opportunity to strengthen consumer protection by:

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Distinguishing between exploitative practices and responsible innovation;

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Avoiding sweeping definitions that inadvertently penalise ethical design;

section_path: Acknowledge Platforms with Robust Age Controls
Conclusion
paragraphs.text: Bumble Response | Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act | October 2025

section_path: Bumble
paragraphs.text: Recognising and rewarding good actors whose business models prioritise user well-being.

section_path: Bumble
paragraphs.text: We look forward to working with the Commission and stakeholders to build a digital future that is safe, fair, and empowering, not only in principle, but in practice.
